summary: I was born in El Paso, Texas, on June 17th, 1919. I left El Paso looking for work after The Great Depression hit, which was a tough time for many people across the country. Eventually, I made my way to Northwest Indiana where I later got drafted into the Army.

The Great Depression was a hard time for everyone, and it was right after that period that I came looking for work. It affected the whole country, and many people, including myself, had to move around to find jobs. It was during this time that I left El Paso and eventually ended up in Northwest Indiana.

I was drafted into the United States Army on April 12th, 1941, and served in the infantry. I stayed with the same company throughout my service, which was part of the 38th Infantry Division. My Army experience took me from training in the U.S. to fighting in the Philippines during World War II.

I served in the 38th Infantry Division, specifically in the 152nd Infantry, K Company. We trained together from the start and stayed as one unit throughout the war. The division was involved in security duties in Hawaii before moving on to combat in the Philippines.

After being drafted, I went to Fort Benjamin Harrison in Indianapolis for my initial processing and swearing-in. From there, I was sent to Camp Shelby, Mississippi, for my infantry training. Fort Benjamin Harrison was the starting point of my military journey.

I trained at Camp Shelby in Mississippi with the Indiana National Guard. The training was tough, especially in the hot swamps, and we did maneuvers in Louisiana, Texas, and Florida. Camp Shelby was where I learned the skills that prepared me for combat overseas.

While training at Camp Shelby, we were sent with the Indiana National Guard. They were part of our unit during the preparation phase before we went overseas. The Guard helped shape our infantry skills and readiness for the war.

During our training period, we went on maneuvers in Louisiana. It was part of the preparation exercises before heading overseas. Louisiana's environment helped us get ready for the tough conditions we would face later.

Texas was another state where we conducted training maneuvers while preparing for combat. Along with Louisiana and Florida, Texas provided a variety of terrain and conditions to get us ready. These exercises were crucial before we deployed overseas.

We went to Tallahassee, Florida, for amphibious training as part of our preparation. This was important because we were going to be involved in island campaigns in the Pacific. Florida's training helped us get ready for those amphibious operations.

Vince Kimberland was an officer in our battalion who kept a detailed diary of our movements and experiences. He sometimes exaggerated his role, but his records helped me remember dates and events, like when we left for overseas duty on Christmas Day, 1943. His diary was a useful reference during our service.

New Orleans was the port where we embarked on December 30th, 1943, to head overseas. We loaded onto the troop ship SS Manta Ray there and started our journey to the Pacific. It was a memorable departure point with a band playing and lots of anticipation.

Our ship passed through the Panama Canal on the way from New Orleans to Hawaii. It took about 11 hours to get from New Orleans to Panama, and then we transited the canal. This was an important leg of our journey to the Pacific theater.
cues: route from New Orleans to Hawaii

summary: We arrived in Hawaii after about 17 days at sea, landing around mid-January 1944. We stayed there for about six months, doing guard duty and rotating around the main island, Maui. Our division was responsible for security, and we relieved the 20th Infantry of the 6th Division while training there.

After training in Hawaii and New Guinea, we went to the Philippines where we fought in the war. We landed in Manila and operated around Subic Bay. The Philippines was where most of our combat action took place during the latter part of the war.

We landed in Manila when we arrived in the Philippines. It was one of the main locations where we were stationed and fought. Manila was a key city in the campaign, and we spent significant time there during the war.

Subic Bay was another important location in the Philippines where we operated. It was part of our area of responsibility during the campaign. We moved through there as part of our fighting and security duties in the islands.

We passed by Hickam Air Force Base when we arrived in Hawaii, but we were not stationed there. Hickam was a large Air Force installation near Pearl Harbor. Our unit was focused on infantry security and training on the islands rather than air operations.

The SS Manta Ray was the troop ship we sailed on from New Orleans to Hawaii starting December 30th, 1943. It was a former medicine liner, and the food on board was the best I had ever eaten. We spent about 17 days on that ship during the voyage.

On New Year's Day, 1944, while on the SS Manta Ray, we sailed down the Mississippi River and out into the Gulf of Mexico. It was part of our journey heading toward the Pacific, and we still didn't know exactly where we were going at that time. It was a memorable start to our long voyage.

After sailing down the Mississippi River, we entered the Gulf of Mexico on our way to the Pacific. We were still unsure of our final destination as we traveled through the Gulf. This was early in our voyage aboard the SS Manta Ray.

When we arrived in Hawaii, we relieved the 20th Infantry Regiment of the 6th Infantry Division. They had been responsible for security before us, and we took over those duties while continuing our training. It was part of the rotation of units on the island.

The 6th Infantry Division was the unit whose 20th Infantry Regiment we relieved in Hawaii. They had been doing security and training duties before we arrived. Our division took over their responsibilities as part of the island's defense.

On July 10th, 1944, we reached the island of Funafuti during our transit to New Guinea. It was a stopover point on our way to the combat zones. The island was already secured, so there was little action there, and we conducted patrols while we were there.

We passed by the New Hebrides on our way to New Guinea. It was part of the route we took in the Pacific, but we did not stop there. It was one of the many islands we passed during our journey to the front lines.

We arrived at Oro Bay, New Guinea, on July 17th, 1944, where we unloaded troops and supplies. It took about three days to get all the supplies off the ship. Oro Bay was where we began to rebuild our fighting strength by bringing in new men and preparing for combat.

The Austin Daly was a cargo ship we boarded on December 1st, 1944, to sail for the island of Leyte in the Philippines. It was part of our movement closer to the front lines as we advanced in the Pacific campaign. This ship carried us into the next phase of the war.
